speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Willa Cummings Carlson has had a lifelong interest in local life, especially that of Troy and her Native American Ridge. As a child she heard and remembered the adults' conversations and she passed many of the stories on to her children, including Mavis Lee Utley, who takes part in several of these talks. Mrs. Carlson speaks of the strange disappearance of a buttermaker in early Troy, of the strange appearance in Troy of a tall veiled woman, of church socials, and of some of her experiences with predicting the future.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, I don't know. Sometimes I wondered if that didn't originate in the mind of my husband, but then of course it didn't. I think it didn't. My husband was not given to fantasizing or hallucinating, and he'd have a fit, a real -life fit. In case you heard me, could hear me saying that he might have fantasized this. But nobody else ever knew anything about it or ever said anything about it, except of course that every time, or for a little while after that, he did tease Shirley Michael Buss about it. Shirley Michael Buss still lives. And if I could see him, I might ask him, but then you know so. A lot of people forget all those things. They can't even remember. Oh yeah, they got a dim recollection. And some things, you know, that were just absolute. Now just for example, I had a friend that was ten years older than I. Well, there weren't any little girls around me, and there were no girls her age around her. And still we were neighbors, very close neighbors. Of course, practically. Of course we lived in the country, but practically next door. So we went together a lot to school and different places. I mean to Sunday school, not school. To Sunday school and different places, and went together. And it made me appear a lot older than what I was at being an older company. But she ran with a younger company altogether, because she went with more, they were more my friends than they were her friends. Well, the minister, the parsonage, was quite a gathering place for the young people in the neighborhood. And we had a little church, a little country church. That's where I learned my first Sunday school lessons. And I think it's where I first learned my lessons, although I did not learn the Bible like I should have. And I didn't get that until later, until I had taught myself. And found that inside I was a very religious person, but it dates back to many things that happened out there in that little church. And, but the, when there was a minister there living in the parsonage, the parsonage would be quite a gathering place, excuse me. He ate too many cookies. And so one Sunday, they were having revival meetings. I don't know whether you were used to them or not, though. This Methodist church was a South Methodist church, and they had gone past, far past the Holy Roller stage and all that sort of thing, with which the early Methodist church had been associated in some communities, but never in that one. And, but they did do a little shouting, and some people went to those meetings just in case somebody got warmed up enough. They said it, that's the way they expressed it. I wouldn't want to be quoted on that, though. To get up and shout. I would not want that, given that was my quotation. But, there'd always be big crowds at these, and the little church was just overcrowded. So we went down there Sunday, and they'd had the people up at the morning's bench, and the meeting had extended far into the afternoon, really, you know, about toward two o 'clock or something like that. And here we were supposed to come back that evening early at six. They'd move the Ephrata League, or the Young People's Meeting, up to six o 'clock so that it would be over with before the services began, and we'd have double services, you see, another service. During revivals, we didn't have that ordinarily. So, Thurman was the name, I'll never forget him. And he was a great, big, portly fellow. Thurman was the name of the presiding elder. And of course, we always had a presiding elder around, you know. Always came to the revival. The name of the minister was the party. And he was not an educated man. He was self -educated, and I don't know how far he'd gone to school, maybe through grade school, I imagine. I don't suppose he'd even had high school, but I never questioned it at that time. But he had a big family, and I liked his family. And his daughter, one of his daughters, Maude, married a man on the lower end of America named Darity. Parents, Darity. And they had no children of their own.

words: Yeah.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Oh, is that right?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, I suppose so, because Maude was, I knew Maude quite well.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: So what happened?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: They're real good friends. Maude and I are, yeah.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I'd like to go to see her. You know, by the way, Maude, I could tell you something. And I'd like to go over here to see Maude, down in Juvie Adams sometime.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Where do you think we can?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I think I can draw Maude out for a lot of things. But, I can stimulate her memory. But, so, yesterday's party was a very genial man, and he said, well, we can accommodate them. He hurried up and had a conference with his wife, and she was a pleasant thing.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: She was a pleasant woman. And Karma looks like that aunt. That's why, I think that's one reason why I liked Karma, because I did like Mrs. Departy, and Mrs. Departy, I thought was such a pretty little woman. And she was small. She was a pretty little woman. Well, they had a cute little boy. He was named Earl. And then they had a little girl named Twyla, but she doesn't belong in the story. And she says, Earl. And Earl, I suppose, must have been about five or six. He didn't go to school, but he was, I might have been six years old about that. And, for a little bit of seconds, eyes just snapped. And then they had a red -headed boy, twelve or thirteen, and he had a lot of fun in him and mischief, but it had been toned down. He was twelve or thirteen, you know, he'd been disciplined. Earl was undisciplined. And, so, the part of the church was a little bit of a little house. It didn't look so little to me at that time, but my thinking back to it, I know it was not a small house, and it must have, poor little Mrs. Departee, must have been hard put to it to crowd all these young people in. But, Mr. Departee, all of you come over to our house, and we'll have the young people over there. We'll get something to eat. We'll have dinner there after a while. And, we'll come back to services. Well, I wondered how they were going to feed us. At that time, it was important to me whether I got to eat or not. Everybody ate so much out in the country. I remember that, that it was an event. So, we got over there, and of course there wasn't room in the kitchen for everybody to work, and I don't know whether it took one or two people who were mighty good kitchen maids, the women, I don't remember who it was, that could help in the kitchen. Well, Ada Harland went, Ada, this was this friend of mine who was ten years older, and she was sitting on a wire davenport. They used to have wire davenports. The Departees had a couple of them. They used them as beds. They were always having to put up somebody, you know, preachers and one thing or another going through the country. They were always putting up at the parsonage, and of course they were always having to make up emergency beds. So, they had two of those cots. I know they brought in one from another room. So, they had them. So, they had them. So, they had them. Yes, we had them. Yes. We had day beds. I got a day bed when I went to school, and didn't know I was going to have to use it after I got married, but I did, and it came in handy. I have an acoustic piano for it to have it. Yes, one of those. Well, they had two of them in there. There was one there, because they were regular in Davenport, and then they got another one in from some other room and distributed it around the house. Well, Ada was sitting on one of them. I was on the same one Ada was, but I was on the other end of it. And we were just a little squeezed in like sardines on that, because we had two to even begin to get them all seated. Pretty soon, Thurman was a great storyteller, as well as being a minister. He was liked pretty well. And this great big fellow, you know, he had a big, deep voice, and he rumbled out and telling stories. We were all interested and all looking at her and Thurman. When all at once Ada, who was a great big fat dress, made over 200 pounds, and you know, when she got up, she made that old Davenport squeak, you know, and two or three of them all went down on the other end. And Ada jumps, and jumps right out in the middle of the door, and she got, it's funny to me yet, when I think of it, it just, it just convulses me. I can't see why anybody that was there could ever forget it. And she jumped right out in front of Reverend Thurman, and she yelled, Jesus, lover of my soul!

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Jesus, love, love, love of my soul. Jesus, Jesus, lover of my soul. And her, her, my word, and she used a lot of home, she was very familiar with it, and when she needed a word to express herself, you know, she didn't know her like some of the boys would have, an oath, but Jesus, lover of my soul. And she'd write in front of the Thurman when she administered it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, that was funny. An old Fred Strom, kind of a flat -headed Dutchman.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: A German guy was going at the time with Eva Stanford, and Eva Stanford was a precise old maid, and very precise. And I don't know what she'd have done if the same thing had happened to her, but she was a very good lady.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: And of course we all were afraid, what in the world is the matter?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: But that time the party had dragged out Earl from under the bed, from under the tavern floor. Nobody had seen him go under, but he was under there. And she said, somebody grab my leg!

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, that was so funny, I had such a crowd. And even to this day, I would have been convulsed at this day, if that motley crowd had been in a room, were in a room now, and that happened, it would just tickle me to death. I just laugh and laugh, I don't know if I can get over it. Well, it subsided after a while, and Earl was given a talking to and promised a punishment. And Ada was told, Ada was somewhat placated by the parties who were ashamed of it, and Ada was mad. By that time her face was red, and the harmond had been affronted, and they were always very particular of their feelings. And of course it always did tickle me when something like that happened. I just couldn't get over it. But we got her calmed down, and I talked to her, and she decided that she'd stay and go back to services that night. Oh, she wasn't, she was going right home for a while. We talked to her and talked her out of it. Oh, that was funny. And then, you know, well time goes on, and Ada and I didn't see much of each other. And I went away to school, and when I came home I'd see her, of course I'd see her because we were neighbors. But I didn't see much of her, and eventually she married, but not real early in life. She was late in life getting married. She must have been 27 or 8, which was late in that rural community. And she married and went out close to Moscow to live at first, then they came back to American Ridge. And she lived over there, back to the old home place, and her husband had farmed it for a while. And then her husband died, and her health broke, and they took her out to the home. She went out to the home, and that's where she died here, oh, two or three years ago.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Oh, Ada?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Ada, yes. And she was out there when I went, or stayed those three months. But Ada was there. And of course she was tickled to death to have me come out there. It was only about a year ago when she died, I think, at 87. Maybe it was a year ago. Yes, I guess it was a year ago. Well, time means nothing to me anymore. It's all drawn together. But anyway, she was tickled to death to see me, naturally. She hadn't seen me for a long while. And they were new friends that she'd made, and she'd made a few good ones out there, which was what she needed to do. She wasn't an easy friend -maker, you know. And she just told everybody, of course she'd lost her, some of her marbles. And like we all eventually have to do, and it's a very sad thing, and an awful sad Ada. But she knew me perfectly. And she couldn't recall anything now. Oh, well, it's impossible for her to recall anything. But you know, ten years ago or something like that, I asked her if she remembered that. Well, no, she couldn't remember the trouble.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: She said, how can you?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: You just imagined that, Willa. And I said, imagine it, nothing. I said, I'll never, I couldn't imagine that. And I said, if I tried, I couldn't imagine the picture of Earl DePartee standing there like a little shrunken shrimp, you know, and his father with his hand on his shoulder, and Earl scared to death. And then he'd look up and see everybody laughing, and he'd have to laugh too, because it was funny. And he'd have to give a laugh too. And Mr. DePartee'd have to cover up his mustache, and cover his... Yeah, I bet he was glad he had a mustache that day, covered part of his... He just couldn't keep from laughing. And there I was, ha ha ha ha, you know, that great big voice of his. He never heard his big voices, honey, I'm sure. We'll get back to the Vale Lady, I think that's where you started out.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Oh yeah, what about the Vale Lady? What is the story about the Vale Lady?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: There is no story, because there can't be a story, because... Oh, sign the microphone.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Am I talking into that now?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yeah. No. Yeah.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I don't want to talk into that. Oh, don't let it bother you. That's the only way I can get your stories down without having to try to write them, which is much too bad. Well, I'd better write them.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I'd like them better, they're written, I'm not doing that. Ha ha ha. You tell a good story. You tell the events experiences real well, Mother. I don't think you need to worry about that. Well, I don't. And besides, this is such a queer thing. My husband, of course, it wasn't anything new, that he'd go to town and never know when to come home, because he was a great talker, he's worse than I am. If that can be possible. But he'd sit and talk and talk and talk and talk and talk and forget. And I have one son, oh I have more than one son, I have two sons there, I can't, that can talk and talk and talk. But at least one of them lets his wife know where he is, and the other one doesn't. He's like my husband. Oh well, she'll know that I'm home when I get there. And I waited and waited one night, oh it was a rainy, stormy night, and I didn't like it a bit. Oh, I did not like it that he was out. And I seemed haunted by everything, and it was, to make it worse, it was a heavenly night. I don't remember what had taken him to town, but he could go on the least excuse imaginable to get to town. When he came home, of course, I was angry and he knew I'd be angry, I sat up and kept the light burning.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: And one of the boys, I think, was this when we lived down Burnt Ridge?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes. The first time. And our car was new. Talking about one of the boys, it had to have been Paul Jr., I was staying home to Paul Jr., he didn't want me to go out with Paul Jr. Because I might give him a cold. He might go and get the flu or any other terrible thing, or a heart attack, and bring it home for me to take care of. Or to give to us the flu. But he'd brought it home, and he'd always have an excuse for that. But if I took the baby out, why, we'd be sick. Because of that. One month after that we got sick, why, it would be because I'd taken him out. But then I don't remember, I don't want him to go. 1918 or 1919. When Paul was a baby. Paul was born in 1918, he wasn't a tiny baby at this time. But Dad didn't buy the car until 1919, spring of 1919. So this would have been that Halloween following, I suppose, maybe. Maybe it was 1919 or 1920.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: One of the two. I don't know that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: So he came home, waited in light. Well he came home a way light, and I was waiting to find out why and when he had to come home. Well he always liked to pick up some big things that had happened, that kept him busy. And I was kind of used to that too. But he said, well sir, I stayed, I didn't have to admit it, but I just had to see how something came out. He was in there with the boys, and that meant that he was in Dick Condell's garage. A fellow named Dick Condell had a garage in town, and he was the one that sold his car. It was a Chevrolet, and of course it was a new model, it was a new car. First and only new car he ever had there. It was that car. And he had working for him a fellow named Michael Buss. They called him Shorty because he was a little short fellow. Very short. And kind of heavy. But he was full of the Dickens. They were Norwegian. Of course you've heard a lot about the Michael Buss that came into Troy. They played a big part in their life down there. Because they took an active part, they were involved in everything during the time they were there. And Shorty was the youngest one, and the shortest one of all. They were all kind of short men.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Shorty worked for Bernard, I believe his name was, worked for Dick. And then somebody else worked there. I don't remember now, I don't recall if it was a short friend besides Jerry Michael Buss. But there was another fellow working for Dick, because there were three of them there. And maybe it was at the game, I'm not sure. And they all, you know, ordinarily, we still had an 830 train that came through town. And ordinarily everybody flocked down to the depot to see who came in on the train. That was one of the big things of the day. Go down to meet the 830. Go down to meet the 12 o 'clock, the 1 o 'clock. And every other train that came in, everybody flocked down to the depot. Especially on the day when nothing else was going on. And at night. And, oh, there'd be big crowds down there on the platform waiting for the train to come in. For the two or three people who came in, and of course it was all over town the next day who came in. Especially if somebody new came in or anything like that, or somebody had a relative come to visit. That was news. And that's what made up the news, part of the news of the little town. And there was lots of drama connected to that, believe me. And sometimes very dramatic events came out of those. So, he said that nobody went down that night. Because it was an awful storm. Oh, it was a terrible storm. It was raining and cats and dogs and black and cold and everything else. So, nobody went down or they forgot. And they let the time go by.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Well, did so -and -so go down and get the mail?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And they had a great big cart and they always pushed this. It was kind of like a wheelbarrow. Called it a cart, but they had one great big wheel out in front. It was a wheel like a wheelbarrow. And that's what they got the mail in. And they wondered if so -and -so had gone down to get the mail.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Who did go down to get the mail?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, they had a kind of funny fellow that went down to get the mail and brought it back up. And nobody had seen him go down to get the mail. They had a nickname for him, I can't remember what that was. And nobody knew whether he had gone down to get the mail or not. But somebody said, oh, of course he did. He never did miss. So, they waited for him to come by. And he did come by. And one or two other strikers that had dared to go down. And then a woman. A great tall woman. Paul said she was taller than he was. That's my husband. And he was 5 '10". 10 and a quarter, I think. At least he was 5 '10". And he said, well, I said, you didn't measure though. No, he hadn't measured exactly, but he could tell. And she had a long black coat on.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: A cloak.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: At that time they called them cloaks and they did coats. She had a long black cloak and he wouldn't know a cloak from a cloak anyway. Anyway, it was a long garment. It was black. And that's all I can vouch for is my husband to know because he wasn't very particular about colors. And Mavis would probably remember that.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Oh, what difference does it make?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: She had a black black veil. And she had a great big black with a big black veil.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Crepe veil?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yeah, he believed it was. He'd come to think of it. Of course, I realized I was putting racism up. It was a thick veil. I said, well, you can tell a crepe veil or a morning veil because it would be crepey. Thick. Yes, he said, you couldn't see her face. Great big veil. He said, a great big one just covered her. And so she came in and stood by the stove and shook her veil. Shook the water out of it. Stood there and shook herself. Of course, it upset her. I don't know if we were here some way and asked her if we could do anything for her. And she said, yes, I want to. You have a garage here.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: She said, do you rent out cars?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, once in a while, she said. Well, she says, I want to rent a car. I want a man and a car. Take me out in the country.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Whereabouts in the country?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: She said, we've got bad roads, you know. It's been raining all evening. It's just been pouring. And I said, you know, we don't have any paved roads down here and our roads are bad. They're pretty muddy. I can't say whether we can go very far in the country or not. But he said, if you go on the main highways, we probably could go a little distance. We won't go far. Well, she said, she didn't know exactly how far it was. But she wanted a man and a car. Take her to Beulah Cemetery. Well, Beulah Cemetery, now you know where Beulah Cemetery is. Above where Aunt Nova lives and that was my sister. Her first husband was buried there. When she died recently, she wanted to be buried at Beulah Cemetery. So that's where she is. She lies up there. And it is in what is known as Big Meadows. I don't know whether you've been around enough to know where Big Meadows is or not. But to us, we called it Above Town. That was a silly way to denote it, I know. But that's what, as a child, I got used to. Everybody said it. Above Town. We were below town. It's above that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: It is above that. You were below town because you were south of town. Yes, because we were south of town.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And you know the old way, you used to think that water couldn't run uphill, so therefore the rivers all had to go south. They couldn't possibly go north. I remember, what a start I got. I said, that river isn't flowing south. I remember that. So she said, she wanted to go. Well, she said, Beulah Cemetery. And somebody else said, my God, woman, nobody can take you out there. That's a terrible place to go. We never could get you there. Well, she said, as far as you can get me. Well, he said, you're going to have to have overshoes and be prepared, George. She said, I have them.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I'm prepared.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, let's see, Dickson. Shorty, it's your turn. Oh, no, no, it isn't my turn. And Paul said, Shorty began to shake and he thought it was going to be his turn and he was going to get out of there. And then he said, besides so and so, I think it was Bill McGahn. But anyway, the other fellow hadn't had it. And by the way, Dick, he said, you haven't gone the last two or three times. It's not my turn. Well, Dick had business that had to be taken care of tonight. Had to be taken care of. He couldn't possibly go. Well, the other fellow couldn't go. Shorty, it's up to you.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: He had to go.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, they'd not do that today. But he wanted to get out. So he got the car out. But when the car did go, he had to have it changed. So they changed it. He started out with it. So then Paul couldn't come home until Shorty got back. So they knew. Well, I said that was a great way. A couple of them got lined up in the back seat to protect Shorty. Instead of waiting her out to see if he'd gone home. They always did have that crookedness 90.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: And we said, would you have wanted me to go out there as a bodyguard?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes. I said, I would have. I don't think about Shorty going out there and being laid out by somebody.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: I said, why didn't you take off her coat so you could see whether she was a woman or a man? Why?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I didn't think about her being.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: I said, did she have a heavy voice or a low, sweet voice?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, she had a kind of a heavy voice, all right, but it was a low, very low voice. I said it was probably a man dressed up as a woman. He never thought of that. Well, I would have always said, you've read too many detective stories. And I acknowledged the fact that I was kind of an addict to detective stories. But I was that too much. So that's what I am. I didn't deny that I was. He said, you've read too many.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: So he took her out?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, he took her out and he got down to the bottom of the hill down there below where you turn up the hill to go to the cemetery, the big hill, almost to it, down below Lundquist. Lundquist House is up on the hill. And down below Lundquist, and he said, now there's a house up there. He said, maybe you can go in there. He said, there's an old man and his son live there. I know that. And I'll take you there. But he said, I can't go any farther. He said, I'm stuck here. I'm going to go back to this fellow that was back of us and get pulled. I'm going to have to be pulled out. Well, he didn't have to be pulled out. But he said that. And she said, all right. And she got out. And he said she went slogging over the mud up the hill.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: And realized that, well, didn't you stay to see if she got up the hill and got to Lundquist?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Or, which wasn't well. I'm tangled up to Sundquist, it was. Well, he said, you got me all confused. I don't remember. And he didn't. He just couldn't remember what she'd done. He got out of there as fast as he possibly could. He revved up the engine and got going. And rolled out. He wasn't stuck. And he came on back. Well, we know one thing. Dick says that anytime we've got a hard show in the country, we've got our man to send. It'll be sure he'll have to go. Because he can take the car anywhere. Well, they were going to watch the paper. And they were all going to watch. So Paul said, they were going to watch to see if they saw anything about anybody having a visitor. And, in the paper. They knew anything about it. And even, Paul said that, when I heard all this, my way of my story was a mystery. She was covered over.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: And she didn't give her name or anything?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Covered completely. No, she didn't give any name. She asked him how much it'd be and she took out the cash and paid him. Right then and there. She said, cash in advance. Oh, Dick discouraged her every way he possibly could. It had to be cash in advance and he did make it an awful big sum of money. I don't remember what it was, but it was a big sum of money. And, of course, it took a big sum to go any place in the car in those days out in the country. I don't know how much it was. I don't remember that. That's a detail I don't remember. Well, that's all there was to that. It wasn't much of a story because nothing did come of it. You just vanished. That's what made the story. Yes, for us.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Does Dick Condell still live?

words: No, Dick Condell was killed. Evan Condell was, Eddie Condell was.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Eddie was Dick's boy and Dick's son. Dick had a big family, both Ed and his wife.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Ed died in a car crash.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: He and Mrs. Condell and Eddie Ramsdale started in South Idaho someplace. Pocatello, I think. To a big grange meeting statewide. Or, maybe it was nationwide. It was going to be an unusually big meeting and they felt very, felt as very good delegates, you know. And, in passing the railroad tracks, they got involved in the, I believe it was the Idaho Falls. They got tangled up in the railroad tracks and were hit by a train. Dick was killed. And, she had bruises and injuries. I believe she had a leg broken. But, Ed was driving and the stick on the steering wheel.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Eddie Ramsdale?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Uh huh. It came up through his leg and out at the hip. Oh, it was a terrible thing. And, he was just, they thought he'd be crippled for life.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: You know, Lee was telling me about that. I know Eddie Ramsdale pretty well. She was telling me that you had predicted that something like that would happen to him.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Did she tell you that?

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Well, you know what?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: That's terrible. I'm so ashamed of that prediction.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: She said that it probably gave him that, some of the faith that he needed to know that he would recover.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Who told you that?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, Lee did. I know that he had that terrible accident. Oh.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I don't know.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: You had told him that he would recover.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I don't know whether you're starting the first thing. I don't know why not. They'd run me ragged to tell their fortunes and you know that's absolutely wrong. I believe it's wrong. I know it's wrong. I don't only believe it's wrong. I know it's wrong. I won't do it anymore. I think it's right. I'm fine. Because you're not. I'm not a witch. You just mean you're a witch. And I'm not the devil's own either. I'm not the devil's own either. Just think what it did for me in Salt Lake that night. You called me at the hospital in Salt Lake. I was going to have surgery the next day. I had gone in thinking it was just to have a tube and an ovary removed and all of a sudden they started all kinds of testing on me and running back and forth. I kept asking them why they were taking more blood and the lab technician would just purse their lips and say routine before surgery. And I said yes but it's already been taken and they kept coming back and coming back. You called me that night about 10 o 'clock. And you told me that everything looked very black. However, that it was all a mistake and everything would brighten. And for me not to worry. And by the next morning they were convinced I had a terminal form of cancer and would die within two weeks. Because I was putting on all these funny cells. I remembered you saying that the night before and I thought I'm going to fool them. They did all those bone marrows on me and decided I, well it was firmly established that I didn't have a form of blood cancer at all. And so then they went ahead and operated. Not really what they might find. But anyway there was no malignancy whatsoever. Just infection that didn't fit in. I helped you by the time I told you. They were doing the bone marrows. That's what I hung on to down there. Oh I've always known things. I don't deny that. I do know things. And what I know I know alright. It does come to me. And those things I don't, that come to me. And I can't help and I don't ask for or use cards or anything like that when I'm not doing that. I think that may be alright. But I don't think I, but I.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: It's funny I don't think, see that as anything wrong at all. I think it's part of the life of the people. And you know some people have the gift. Very few people do I think. But some do. And I guess what you have you have. I suppose it's not a blessing but it isn't a curse either. Well those things that I say that come to me. That I can't say anything against.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Because I have, those things have come to me. And telepathy I do believe in that. I know it does happen. I have very firm telepathy established with my oldest son. Better with him I think than possibly any of the family. Except Mavis. I do have some telepathy with Mavis. And maybe some with Duane. But with Paul, but I don't have it unless Paul is in holy terror.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: But say speaking of that and the thing that I have. After you call me today. I was going to do a few things. Well I didn't get many of them done until I went into the bedroom. And then I picked up these things and got into that envelope full of notes. And got to reading those notes and those people and seeing how early they died. And I just get lost in that stuff.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Always.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And that happened to me in there. And I just thought I've got to get rid of this now. I can't have this out here now. I've got to do a few things and get myself dressed. So it could have been from the trailer next door. Or even next door to that one. And everybody had their windows open today. And so the voices were caring. But somebody says, Mother. Mother! And I know that boy. And it was either Paul or Duane.

words: I thought, my goodness.

You know. When it looked closed. And he'd be coming around. I thought of all those things. Got the window but I didn't see a soul. And then I came here and I thought. Well, I needed to get up. And that might have been the way of making me get up from there. I'm controlled of course by a lot of things.

words: Did you think of me? About something?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley

words: And you didn't have any word from Paul or Duane?

words: Well, I had to get busy. So I wouldn't let myself think, ponder on that. And it had slipped my mind until now. But that was very much present for a little while. But it was such a kind of a joyful voice. Just like they were tickled. Mother is like Paul had come in. Mother, I'm home.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And they both say mother. Well, Duane's quit now and says mom a good deal. And Bruce says mom. But Keith and Paul, Paul Jr. and Keith, they say one word and that's mother. They never call me anything else. Neither in writing nor in voice.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: So one of them had a message today but it was a joyful message. It was good. See, I know the atmosphere. And I get those things.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: What was it that you were telling me about the guy that was missing? Was it Ed's help?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, Old Mankind's. How you help locate him. No, I didn't get to help locate him. But I did something alright. It worried me half to death. This Old Mankind's had worked for us and I knew what kind of a man he was. I knew that the poor old man. He was, I don't know whether it was from age and he was aged young or not. But he was a pretty good looking man in the face. Physically he wasn't a bad looking man. And he had that beautiful daughter. She really had a beautiful face in one way. Beautiful complexion and good features. But no expression there. It wasn't an intelligent face. And her husband took care of everything. Took care of the kids. I think they had five girls.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Five daughters.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: They lived up there in that sundale house for a while. And his name wasn't Kimes, of course. Kimes was her father. Yeah, their name was Pierce. Oh yes, Pierce. And that little, the oldest girl. And she was getting to be such a handy girl. Reminds me of Star. Just a beautiful child. She could just do everything. She was learning to do all that at her father's. Daddy was teaching her, she said. And Daddy just taught her all kinds of things. So she was learning to take care of the mother. Daddy would do all her housework and everything. But the mother was around with him and talking with him and playing with him like one of the girls.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: It's funny.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And Kimes went to work for Agramstown, I wonder. Cutting wood. It always gave employment to a lot of fellas that were hanging around town. Didn't have any place to go. He'd give them a grub stake and tell them they could climb and axe whatever tools they needed. And tell them they could go up in the hills and there were cabins up there. And of course they could go out and chop plenty of dead wood around anywhere they wanted. And burn it and there's an old stove in there.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: A cabin and so on.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And they could make a living that way through the winter. And frequently did. And he nearly always had four or five guys up there cutting wood for him. And that's the way Ed's made his money and he's been a good. He knows exactly how to make it off of the fellas.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Exactly.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: He's a regular fella. And, oh they used to drive me crazy to read their poems and everything. Tell them to buy cars I think. And I had told Ed's fortune. And old man Kimes was in town and got a sack of groceries. And left town. And there was a snowstorm hit and he got lost. He never even got to the cabin. So then somebody went around horseback or however they went around to the cabins to find out if they were alright. Because Ed didn't like to leave them up there and not keep track of them. So he found out that Kimes had been in town and had wandered out in that snowstorm. He wasn't a... he could take care of himself in one way but he wasn't a man that would plan ahead.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And Ed knew this. And so he got raving. He heard that he wasn't at his cabin and he hadn't been seen since that time. And there were no tracks leading into the cabin. And Ed got excited and got a posse out to look for him. So the men just as soon as it cleared, the snowstorm cleared away, the men all went to looking and they looked for several days. And failed to find him. Well, Paul my husband was in town and he heard about it. Well Ed came in and Ed was worried. And he came to Paul and he said, I guess I believe he said he promised me that he would never bother me again about telling him all this stuff. So I didn't make him promise those people. They wouldn't come to me again. I didn't want to feel like a gypsy. But anyway, he wanted to know of Paul if I had ever located anybody.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: And who did Ed think I located? Who was it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I don't remember.

words: But I said, well why don't they look? When they were looking for somebody, why don't they look somewhere?

words: Paul said, you said why didn't they look for him and that's where they found him. And well, I said I wasn't locating him. I wasn't thinking of that. I said I was just using common sense because most of this locating people is common sense. And cutting away the rubbish. The little foolish things. Like I've been doing today now. Get off on other routes instead of the one that I'm supposed to stay on on that road. And I said that's what most of this is. A lot of it. I said maybe some of it is really prescient. Something that comes from outside.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: But much of it's that.

words: But when I went to bed that night, could I think of anything else but old man kind?

I said I don't know whether I went to sleep or not. Either I've had an awful real dream or I haven't been asleep. Seems to me I haven't been asleep. But I said you can go to town tomorrow and tell Ed to look up. They've been looking in the wrong spot. I said instead of looking around on the ground for kites, look up in the tree. If, as they think, the coyotes might have come and howled, somebody had told us that they were out in force. I said he might have been afraid. I said he would have done the first thing. And he would have done the simple thing. And that would have been right for anybody that was lost in the hills. If you follow, and I said he's such a natural man because he didn't have a lot of forethought, human forethought, he'd have climbed that tree.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes you did.

words: My kids wasn't around too much. There'd be a fire under the tree. They'd find evidence of a fire under the tree. You told them the location of the tree. Yes, I did remember that. I did say that. There's been a fire. And they'd find his body in two pieces. Yeah, I told them that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I did say two pieces. You were right there, by the way, you had to be snooping around. Always listening to the NBC things before you go and have a chance.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: That's to freshen your memory.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well anyway, he went to town intending to tell us. But when we got to town, the news was that they had found his body underneath the tree. And it was broken and the coroner was out. And about that time, I don't know whether the coroner was in town already or whether he came through town soon afterwards.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Troy.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I thought they went out on your information. No, not mine. They'd already done it. Thorval Haverberg had had a terrible dream. Well he had dreamed that there was a fire, a tree of fire. And that he heard the coyotes howling. And he dreamed that he got up and went to hunt for the tree and all he could hear was the coyotes howling and he'd come back. And he had decided it was a dream. But the next morning he knew it was more than a dream. So he got somebody to go out with him and they'd look over there. He'd seen a place where the animals had dug. And they got up there where the animals had dug and got out some of the groceries that had fallen. And a limb was broken. And the limb had broken and gone into the ground. And so they looked there and the tree had been a fire and the fire had run up the side of the tree. And the fire had caused the limb to break. And that is what they deduced from what they saw. And they moved that and found that the body was there and they caught help to get it before. And when they got there the body was broken in two. And they reasoned that the old man was sitting up against the tree and it caught fire by his smoking. That's what they thought I think. And that was the fire he'd built underneath who wore it. Oh yes, I guess that was it. He'd built a fire and the fire got away from him and entered the tree. Burned up the trunk of the tree to a certain distance to this dead, big dead, partly dead limb. Partly dead. Made it easier to catch fire. And he woke up and went to run to get away from it. And the limb had caught him across the back and pinned him to the earth and took his back. And that was the end of it. And so they never thought of anything else. And I said, but didn't they think of booking?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And Herman Reuberg said, by gosh they didn't. I don't know what Herman was doing there with Paul, but he was over there with Paul. Hilda of course believed that I wouldn't do anything. And Herman said, oh my gosh, Paul, they didn't. They just made up their minds of that limb. No, I said he didn't. He didn't. He wasn't crushed by the limb. He was on that limb. He was trying to get away from the fire. By the coyotes or something. He was trying to get away. And he hung over the limb and the limb broke.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And of course he went on both sides of the limb and fell into this. There were five feet of snow under the tree. He went into the snow and the limb went along with him. And being charred, it broke all to pieces. They said the limb was in many pieces and Herman was there.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Now is Herman still living?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No, Herman's dead. Oh he is, because I thought Sam didn't. Yes, he could have been good. And Hilda would have been. Herman and Hilda would have both been.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Hilda's living. But she wouldn't be near as good without Herman because Herman knew about Burnt Ridge and Hilda never lived down there. She was old enough to work. She went to Spokane to work. And it would only be as Herman would bring back things. And then she would recall what Herman had told her and the Rootburgs had told her. Yes, she could speak pretty well for the Rootburgs, but she wouldn't be as good.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No, no, no, I don't want to do this.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No, I don't want to be remembered for that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I don't want that kind of notoriety. I would like it myself.

words: It doesn't seem like notoriety to me. It seems like a lost art to me.

words: It may be, but why did I catch up on a lost art?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I'm afraid of these lost arts. Well, it just seems to me it's the art. Like some people have great scenes. The art that I missed was... It wasn't his voice, that his body was the instrument, but his singing voice was a gift from God.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Who is this?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Mario Lanza. Oh yes, well many people know that, that it's a gift from God. I know this, that... Well, my body was an instrument or something like that. Oh, Mays. Shame on you. You never tried to make it so. I tried to teach it to you, but... But you don't really try to make it so. If you became conversant with the Bible, and don't misunderstand me to be saying that I am, because I know only too little about it. But yet, I know that everything is in the Bible. Everything. Everything is there. For instance, here's one little verse, but oh it contains so much. Seek and ye shall find. Knock, and it will be opened unto you. Ask and ye shall receive. Now, well I know that. I believe in it. Maybe. You keep trying to convince me of something, and I'm already... Uh huh, I know, but... But believing it and accepting it as a part of living are two different things. I always believed it too, Mavis, but it never did me much good. Now I've... Well it's done me a wonder of good. Once in a while somebody has asked me a question that I've not known exactly how to answer. And instead of cudgeling my brain, like I've always done, because I always thought I had a pretty good brain. I don't. I am an instrument. I realize that.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: And I've just said, oh dear God, what will I say?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And suddenly the answer has come out. And it's a wonderful answer. But I wasn't the author of it.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Did, um, I'm just really curious, where did you learn what you're telling me from?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I didn't learn it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I just picked it up.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: You didn't have anybody teach it to you?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, I knew a nurse in Lewiston.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: All my life long I've collected characters. Most people are just people, that's all they are. But now and then you meet a character, and I know them when I meet them. And I've met characters all along life's way, and when I've needed a friend, I've always had that brain pop up. But it's a shame I haven't done anything with myself. I could have done much more. And it just makes me sick to think that I haven't finished up a lot of work that I should have done. And I know that I'm hanging on now, because there's something I have to do. And I don't do it. In my crippled hands I could learn to crochet and do a lot of things, like other people do. But I start and then I can't do it.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: What makes one of these people a character?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I mean, that really makes them stand out.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Don't you know? Can't you tell what makes a character?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Now you've been around, that's what you've been trying to do, is collect characters.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: The way a person lives makes a character. Of course some people are funny without knowing it. And we call them characters too, of course. Sometimes the term is used loosely to denote different things. We don't always mean the same thing by it. Some characters are, we say that, well he's a funny character. Possibly we say that because he's so absurd and makes a lot of, comes to a lot of absurd conclusions. And so we laugh at him, and because he causes laughs, we say he's a character. Humorous character, perhaps so. Well, he's a solemn looking owl. Yeah, he is that. He's a solemn character. That we assign some trait to him. Some characteristic trait as characterizing the man himself, or the child or something, whatever it is. I think we do that. When some, I think it is mainly that, that if some characteristic predominates to the extent that it dominates the personality of that person, then we say it is a character.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Only if you, only certain people stand out like that. Most people don't. Most people do not.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Most people make up the common ordinary level. We have to have that level around. So that the, that the characters, as I call them, but the outstanding people can stand out.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: What makes that man or woman stand out?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: If it were not for the contrast with the people who are somewhat blah, he wouldn't stand out. If you get a bunch of these stand out people together, well, afterward you'll say, well I don't know who was the most outstanding in the crowd. It seemed to be a group of people on quite a level. They were a high level, but they were all on a sort of a level.

words: Oh, she was a nurse.

words: And, yes, no one else was in the garage that night. There was a bunch of fellas in there that were laughing and having a great time about, about Shorty and the Halloween ghosts, you know. Or he took this ghost.

words: Did this ghost go up there?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: To meet another ghost. Yes, to meet another ghost up there in the cemetery.

words: Was it that time of year?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: It was Halloween.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And on a stormy night, you know, the setting was just superb.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: That's what I said immediately. My goodness, this ought to be right. I don't understand what, what, why is it that you're so critical of fortune telling as something, I don't understand why you don't like it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, I think it's involved in, especially now, there's a wave going across the country. That and a lot of other things. And devil worship. And these devil worship covens that they have, they do this fortune telling. And they have. They use cards of symbols and they go through with them and a lot of other things. A lot of horrible things have been done in the name of it. Well, I think of it as an old time skill, is the way I think about it.

words: Yeah, as something that, that there's a tradition, you know, there's a tradition of it. That, I mean, you learn what, you learn the basics of it from somebody else and that's something that's passed on. Oh, I don't know whether it was passed on or not. As I say, I knew this nurse.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And she'd had a story, a sad story in her life. She's a very good looking woman and I went down there to board at this boarding place. And it was gotten for me by a friend, a man whose daughter was, he was a mail carrier, in fact, up there where I taught my first school. And he knew I was looking for a boarding place. I thought I was going down to take this last quarter of school and I was missing two weeks of it and there's only nine weeks in the whole thing. Seven weeks is as long as I'd be there. I thought, well, my money might hold out for that. I was going to summer school besides. Oh, that's the way I went to school. When I got out, I went all the time, summer and winter, and all the time in between if I could. But money, of course, was always a problem. And I had to have enough to go on. But I thought it would be nice to go to Lewiston and pay my board for once. And it didn't last, but I went down to get the place this man told me about where his daughter was. I got the place, they had room for another girl, I had boarded with Rose. I was perfectly willing to board with her. I found out it wasn't a good scheme afterward, but I didn't know then. And Mrs. Clark was there, sort of helping the woman, and Mrs. Cameron, who ran the boarding house. And just a little house, it wasn't much of a house, and she only had this one room to rent. So she took in the two girls, but she ran a boarding house, as I say, for people downtown who had to have their meals out. And the beaches were building a new building, a new house for themselves on the hill. That was the big store in Lewiston at the time. The beaches had the big store. Well, it became Carl Anderson's store. And now it's a Bon Merchet. It's a Bon Merchet. It's had quite a story too. But it was beaches, and that was the big store, and the expensive store, and all this and that. Mr. and Mrs. Beach were some people. And Mr. and Mrs. Beach and their son, Hal. No, no. And then Mr. Beach, who ran the RC Beach, ran the store, had a brother who was a shoeman, he was a very nice fellow named Hal Beach. And they had a couple of girls, but the girls weren't there. Hal Beach was there occasionally, to come up to the RC. But anyway, this woman seemed to know all about all the nice things. And she'd get out her nice lemon, her nice silver and one thing or other that she had with her.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: And I wondered, so, I remember once I went in to pay, paid by the week, I went in to pay, I said, well, which one of you guys should I pay?

words: Cameron had.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley

words: But, and she was mysterious and everything else, she was everything else that I wouldn't really want to be. And yet I loved her because she was different. Just Mrs. Clark. words: So, I just, from little things, as she said, Aunt Nora never did let, if she ever told them the cards, she never let me say it. I never did say it. She didn't know anything about it. But, her tea leaves and her coffee grounds, I did know that Aunt Nora did it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I'd seen her in a little town.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: So, I've seen that, but I was like Mavis, a kind of a sleeper on such things as that, you see.

words: So, it runs in the family.

words: Mavis has seen a lot of things that I didn't realize she did, you see. She tells me every now and then that she heard things like that. So, I suppose that was it. But, then about this fortune telling with cards, oh, I thought that was great. I was going to learn to do it right away. So, I did go over there. One time I went over and washed a horrible stack of dishes. Mrs. Clark called me and I was working. Mrs. Eastwood, by the way, came, my old friend, came and wanted me to come stay with him.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: What did I come to Lewiston and go and board out for?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: If I wanted to board, I could stay over there and my board wouldn't cost me anything. Well, I wouldn't board that way with anybody. She was a very generous woman, Mrs. Eastwood was. So, I went over. She had a girl working for her and I didn't want to rival that girl. Well, she said, Agnes wants to go all the time and so now I'll stick her with, so she can go two nights a week. She hadn't always let her, but she'd let her juggle them around. I'd be willing to stay a couple of nights a week with the kids. Well, of course, I was willing to stay because I stayed in most of the time. I didn't get around tonight. I was getting my lessons. And I did that and I stayed with the kids. And I sewed them. I went to sew them. Made little boy suits. She had a pattern and I went with a pattern and I sewed them up on the machine. I never made little boy suits in my life, but I suited Mrs. Eastwood on those little pens anyways. Well, I went down the river. They gave an excursion on the John Teal boat. I used to have a picture of it. And I wondered how in the world I'd make the money. Take the dollar for the excursion. I was going to get a dollar to go on that boat. It was more than I knew. And I knew I couldn't afford to eat on the boat. So I said to Mrs.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Eastwood, is it okay if I take a lunch?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, of course, she said, you don't have to ask me that. Well, I said, I wouldn't have taken it without asking. Well, she said, you don't have to ask me. Take two lunches if you want. You have to have some for supper. Well, I took just a little lunch, not much. I didn't want to take much. And I took a little and figured I'd get along all right. But in the meantime, Mrs. Eastwood rented out her rooms upstairs to, well, women around town. And one school teacher named Sylvester, I won't forget her. And so one Sunday afternoon they were lounging around and came downstairs. And I said to Mrs. Eastwood, I told her about Mrs. Clark, about Mrs. Clark reading the book.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: She said, did you catch on to it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, you tell mine. So I told Mrs. Eastwood I could do that pretty easy. She didn't know it, but I knew it. I could tell it to her pretty easily. And of course, the more I told, the more I wanted to tell. And I became very glib. Well, I was on this boat and Mrs. Sylvester, you see, was a penmanship teacher and she was on the boat going up on this excursion. We went up to the mouth of the Grand Ronde River and there we disembarked and made a great ceremony of it. And then we could go in three states, Idaho, Oregon, and Washington, you know, all of it. In a little while. And there were little foot bridges and we went across them and wonder we hadn't broken them down. And then we climbed a hill. I said we could look over into the three states. And we climbed a hill and somebody had a photograph. Well, I suddenly became very popular. I never had been popular. I didn't have pretty clothes. I didn't have, I was lucky to have anything to wear. Although I had better clothes that spring. I taught five months and I had better clothes than I'd ever had in my life when I went to school. And I had better clothes, but I'd never been popular and then I couldn't spend money. I never had any money to spend. I was a big head of it. I couldn't spend money on my own. And I don't pretend that I was ever a popular girl. I was only popular because I could furnish a lot of data that they'd lack awful well to have in some of the classes. And I was smart enough to not be a paper writer in various ways. Not money. But I saw to it that I got the proper kind of pay alright.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: They couldn't snub me. I wasn't the kind of a person that could snub. And so I, I felt better. I thought I was established on a better basis. Anyway, asked neither here nor there.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And uh...

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: You told her fortune that day?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes, Miss Sylvester came and said, I want to ask a special favor of you. And that's to tell my fortune again. But these are friends of mine. Real good friends. She wanted Jack Butler to have her fortune told. And I said, I will not tell Jack Butler's fortune.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: And she said, why not?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And I said, I don't like her. And she said, well you're right about it. Oh come on now, Willa. We are friends, Jack Butler. Well I'll not tell your fortune. And I said, listen to me.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I'll not tell your fortune. You'll be sorry. If I ever do.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: And she said, why? And I said, do you need to ask me why?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: She blushed. She said, no.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: She was a neighbor to Stacey's. And she was a mean one. And I knew about some of her meannesses. And I'm sure that there were a lot of them I didn't know anything about and wouldn't want to know. But she let me alone.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Scared her out.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And I, but I went ahead and told her three. And first thing you know, I was running a wholesale parlor. Sitting there, they got me a table. And I, well I said after a while, I'm not seeing any scenery at all. Here I am sitting here telling fortunes. This is no fun. I'm not having any fun. Well they were giving me stuff for lunch. I could, you never saw such a sack. I was always popular that day because I had so much lunch. I had a big sack of bananas and oranges and everything that they could bring up, you know, because they had them for sale on the ship. And candy bars. Oh I had a great big paper sack just crammed full of stuff. And they all gave me that. And I timed them and only told them a few minutes, you know. That was all for, finally I said well, because I've got all the food I want. I don't want any more food. I'll start charging dimes. So I charged dimes. So finally, the captain of the ship, he thought that was pretty smart. He was up there looking on and several, and all the men that were standing around, hanging around, were on there. One of their fortunes told. But the captain, he said, well I'll tell you, you tell my fortune, and if you hit, you tell my past, and if you hit one, no, he said, you hit two things that are true in my past. And, tell you what I'll do, I'll give you dinner. The best dinner there is at the ship's herbs, he said, on the boat. Well that was fine. And so I undertook it, and I was so sure I could tell him things from the past. And I said, I remember first, I don't remember the captain's fortune, but I told him a number of things, and he said, stop, don't tell me anymore about my past. I know about that, and it's bad enough and you're getting into it. I said, let's go to the future. So I told him a few things, and he said, that's fine. He said, you know, you've come the nearest to hitting things if anybody had ever told my fortune. Well I couldn't believe it. I just couldn't believe it. I could not believe such a thing. He said it like he believed it, and like he thought it was true. He asked me where I'd learned it, and here this was just that same year. The year I'd gone down there for that last quarter, I said I only had seven weeks left of it. And I'd never tried such a thing before. So that's how I got to telling them. It was easy, it was simple. It was like falling off a log. And I don't think it was very smart, and I've never been proud of it. Well, I'd gotten, anyway I got my dollar back. You know, it wasn't long until I had ten times. And I got my dollar back, and I'd been worried about spending that dollar. I think I got a dollar and a half, and then I got my dinner. And sitting at the captain's table, that was the spot, even on the little old riverboat, the John Teal. I'll never forget the John Teal. Now that, he wanted a background in my telling them, and that was it. That's where it started. And then, well, you know, I was always running into it, always running into it. Always and always and always. Then the last year in high school, I came back to Troy, and my folks talked about going to Michigan that year. And I had a real tender feeling toward my kid, the little kid sisters. I always did have, I always loved them. The three of them, they were a lot younger than I was. And they loved me, and they liked to have me come home. And I felt awful sorry for them, because they had a hard time too. My folks weren't very sympathetic to the little kids. And they wanted to go back to Michigan, but they hated to leave the children. Mama said she hated to go off and leave them, and I'm sure she did. And I said, well, she thought I ought to stop school and stay home. No, Papa said no. I had started the school, and he didn't believe in anybody backing out. He was so right. And I certainly agreed with him. And I said no, but I will do this. I'll stay at Troy and take high school. I can do that. I can take the subjects I need in high school this year. And then I'll come out on weekends and stay with the kids. I can come out every Friday night, go back Sunday night or Monday morning. And my brother was big enough to take the buggy, and he said yes, he could take me in on Monday morning. So that was agreed. When they went to Michigan. They didn't go to Michigan. That trip blew up. I didn't think they'd go in the first place, but yet it was just a possibility that they might, so that's how I happened to go to Troy. Well, toward the end of the year, there was a man named Milton who was a very smart guy. And he was out here for his health, out west for his health, teaching by chance in Troy High School. And he did a lot for a lot of people there. Now there were some of his methods that were not good. I know that now from the viewpoint of a teacher. But then he did a lot for a lot of children. And put a lot of children on their feet. And he did a lot of things. He was a very, he was a doctor. He was a trained doctor. He was issued, but he lost his health. And he wasn't trained as a teacher. So he did a lot of unorthodox teaching. But a lot of it was awful good teaching.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: He taught children instead of subjects. There were some kids that came in that couldn't do it. And so he would find out what they could do. And the first thing he knew, they were doing other things. And he'd have them doing work and maybe writing stuff about what they did. And one boy I remember that came in from the hills of Troy. And he was a woodcutter's son. And he was kind of a woodcutter. And he was in trouble with a girl that had said he wrote crazy notes to her. And certainly obnoxious notes. But I don't think he had written them exactly. But anyway, there was an awful fuss about it. And that girl's mother raised a big howl. And goodness only knows that I'm sure that it was fouled up. And he'd gotten expelled. That was from school the year before. I wasn't there. I didn't know about what happened at the time. I was told I could see what happened. Well, Milton took him back into the school and let him take a course in bookkeeping. And he was good at it. An awful good bookkeeper. And he was such a good penman. My goodness, he was a good penman. And it was Linus Carlson. He taught for years.

words: Oh, that's a funny thing, yes. We had at the end of the year an exhibit. And then he taught plays during the year. And he never did cast the plays the way I would have cast them. But you see, I was at the beginning of my career in high school. I wasn't at the end after I'd had several courses in drama and all that stuff. And I have, and I loved it and so on. And I had a flair for it in the beginning. But a flair doesn't give you the knowledge, basic knowledge. Well, he taught me a lot about that. And I got a lot of things from Melton's. And Mrs. Melton taught. She taught algebra. And she taught me algebra. She taught me the easy way to get to algebra. So, as far as that's concerned, Mr. Melton was very good. So, and I didn't suffer. Nor, a lot of my friends didn't suffer. But I thought there were some of the lower classes that he neglected terribly. And some of the basic things that he didn't seem to bother to teach skimmed along on. But for anybody that really wanted to learn, he seemed to have it. And he did teach children. He'd have them there, you come in after school and I'll help you with this and I'll help you with that. If they mentioned that they couldn't get along on something and they needed help, well, Melton gave it to them.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Or Mrs. Melton gave it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: They were very good that way. As I say, they taught children. They had a heart for children. And, but this exhibit, we never had anything like that before. That I knew of. I had nobody else there. And I'd only gone a year before that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: So, for entertainment, he said we should have a fortune teller. That always takes well, he said, at any kind of a school exhibit. And we should have a fortune teller. And always in hotel fortunes. Well, he said we'd have to import a fortune teller.

words: Well, did you know where we could import a fortune teller?

words: I remember our docent scratching his head. The school wag. Scratching his head and saying, well, Dr.

words: Melton, do you know where we could import a fortune teller? At the school? Well, that would cost money, wouldn't it? Who's got the money?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No, Dr. Melton said, I don't think so. He said, I have friends who can tell a fortune and we can import one.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Well, my goodness, can we have our fortune teller?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, sure, he said. Well, I was just as excited as all the rest of them. I'm going to have a gypsy fortune teller. I suppose Mr. Melton knew gypsies. I had no idea that I was the imported fortune teller. So, I was staying overnight in town. I had a place where I would go to work. But I was staying that night with Ellen Schumacher, a friend of mine. We were staying together and he came down to see us.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Mr. Melton.

words: And they told me I was to be the fortune teller. And that they wouldn't take no for an answer. Well, I can't tell fortunes. I said, oh, yes, you can. Some of the kids have told me how you've told fortunes. And so, they drilled me on using a lot of these and making my language a jargon of fractional gypsy.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And I really had it down pretty good. And I didn't forget it all day long. Afterward, I wondered, he believed in hypnotism and I wondered if he'd hypnotize me. I asked him if he had.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: I said, I don't think I can hypnotize, but did you hypnotize me so I could talk that stuff and remember it all day?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley

words: And you get it.

words: He said, that's the way you learned fortune telling, you said. Well, I said, all I did, he said, I don't deny that I suggested to you. But he said, I did not hypnotize you. I was afraid I'd been hypnotized. Because I could go through this routine. Well, they put up a wigwam and put me in it so that I looked unnatural. I was in an unnatural setting. I nearly smothered to death, of course. It got real hot. It was May days, it was early in May. And I got terribly warm in there. And I wore Mrs. Walmer, Mrs. Walmer had all the gay looking petticoats in town at that time. There were lots of petticoats. The women who dressed up around town and wore long dresses, of course. Kids who wore just ordinary dresses halfway between the knee and the shoe top, I guess. They didn't have this many skirts, they didn't have one or two. But the women who would go with the petticoats, they had all changeable silk, was a very common thing to have. Green and red together and blue and green and so on. And Mrs. Walmer had a lot of them. So they said, well we'll go to Mrs. Walmer and borrow her skirts. And one teacher, Mrs. Melton was the one who did that. Mrs. Melton did all that. I don't think any other woman would teach her that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I got dressed up. And I don't know how I got down to the Eagle.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: When I got down to the Eagle.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And I came in early.

words: And I was driven up in Earl Duffy's car. Mr. Melton and Earl Duffy. Earl had no idea who I was bringing up. He was introduced to me as Mr. Duffy. And this was Madame Zephronia, I think. Zephronia is the name of the Z. I told everybody what he wanted to know.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Oh, we'd say, am I going to get married twice?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: The old men would say, the businessmen.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Oh, don't you see a second wife for me?
