words: And I didn't realize what I was saying. And I was sorry, oh so sorry, afterward that I had said what I did to one man. He came in and he was a businessman, a jeweler. And I didn't really realize that they considered he had a very termigent wife. Although I knew that she was kind of a bossy woman.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: That's what we called her when we did those things.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And he said, oh now come, come come. You haven't seen very many things he did yet.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: My hand. Well I took that problem. I didn't tell my father.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: He said, don't you see some nice young wife there for me?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And I understand you don't so and so. He's going to have a second wife and be a nice young girl. I didn't say that when I was not here to discuss his fortune. I said, that means bad luck to you. You have a piece of very bad luck because of asking for that kind of, not your friend. No, I said there is no such thing as a second wife in your hands. I said one wife has been entirely too much for you. Well I didn't really realize what I was saying to him. And I was very sorry after him.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I didn't.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Because I just kidded him and he never lived that down. Nobody liked her.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Were you recognized by many people that day?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I wasn't recognized by anybody. And what made me feel kind of funny was that I wasn't noticed as being absent from school. That really made me mad. I thought I was more of a student than that.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: But toward evening, several people said, what was the matter? Ellen, what does it matter?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, Ellen said she had to go home today. She had to go out home.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Out in the country?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh my goodness. We wanted her for this and we wanted her for that. Suddenly when they realized I was gone, they wanted me for a dozen things. But that was well toward evening of the first day. We had it two days. Don't know why we did, but we did have it two days. And I went the second day dressed up in that. And I was painted. That was another thing I did. I don't think I ever did get clean. I was painted with ochre. Brown ochre. And I was just as brown as anything you ever saw. No Puerto Rican ever looked browner than I did. And then orange colored paint on me. Oh, I was painted up. You never saw such a sight. And strings of beads. And if they'd have noticed, well, they didn't go around. They didn't take anybody's beads, you know, connected with the school. I think Mrs. Melton had a couple of strands of beads that she could take out. But Mrs. Walner dug out some. They kept a store and they had a lot of things. And she entered into it with Mrs. Melton. Was so happy. She'd never been able to do much for school because people left her out of things.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Was this Mrs.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Berta Walner's mother?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Mrs. Melton had a lot of charm about her. She brought in people who had never cooperated with anybody before. The Meltons were great people. And they did get cooperation from all kinds of people. Well, too much cooperation for me. And I didn't dare wear any of my own clothes because I'd worn out all of them so much that they would have recognized them right away. Of course, Ellen was all prepared to say, well, Disney Willows because she took hers home with her. Like that.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Is this Auntie Owen you're talking about?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No, no, Ellen Schumacher. Oh, Ellen Schumacher. Good friend of mine. Yeah.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: When was it discovered in Troy that you could do this?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Because I remember as a kid that you were always lined up in the booth. Oh, yes. Lined up for every Spanish, every program that there was. Always lined me up. And I used to do it. And never quite feeling right about it. And yet not hardly daring to refuse them. I'd done it for one. I'd have to do it for the next social gathering. Well, your booth always earned a lot of money. Yes, I earned lots of money. I earned lots of money for different things. And so I said one time, well, I'm not going to go to Fredime anymore. It'll have to be a quarter. Well, that didn't make any difference. And they said, well, you'd just as well charge a dollar. You'd get a dollar.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: People must have liked pretty well the job.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I remember that Ed Ramsdale told Dad that I told him for ten cents, and he's fortunately told by a lot of people, he even paid out five dollars to gypsies. And I told him more things that he knew were right and true than he'd gotten for five dollars.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Funny.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: That is, he didn't tell me that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And those were times when Dad said, he thought it was alright, I can do it. He never objected to me doing it. He thought it was funny. I never told his. I used to laugh at him. And say, I know that, well, I know my husband does believe in me because he's never allowed me to tell him.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I used to tease Dad. But he never objected. And then, let's see. Well, oh, toward afternoon, I said I want to clean up at noon. So I owe me till noon. I'm getting out of town. Friends are going to pick me up. Okay, so they smuggled me out the back way out of the schoolhouse and we went down over the hill. And we could do that and keep out of sight of the school to go to Ellen's apartment. I got to Ellen's apartment. I got a bath. And she helped me clean up. And I cleaned up. And then I came in. I was here from the country. I just got in.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Why weren't you here yesterday?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: You should have been here. We had a gypsy fortune teller, I know she told me this and she told me that and she told me this and she told me that. All over the house of the kids. And they never knew me. I thought that was the funniest thing. And so finally, somebody told it.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Oh, were you the fortune teller?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: The very idea. No wonder you told me that. You knew all about it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, that was fun. I admit that I had fun at that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: That was fun.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And then after that, all the Spanish clubs and all the clubs. And then I've been at parties and they want me to tell fortunes of the cards. And I remember once at Marvin Chaney's and they were having a little dancing party. You know, dancing games first and then they went in to dance. And everybody left off dancing and they came and stood around and crowded around to hear the fortunes. That's how much attraction it has for people.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: But it isn't good. Well, let's change the subject. Yes, let's do it.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Would you tell us again?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No, no, no. I'm not asking that. I'm afraid. Gosh, no, no.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Would you tell again the story about Amal Watts' disappearance?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: That's such a good story.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: That was his name, right?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes. You got the name right.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, I suppose you ought to have a real old timer who was in the dairy business tell you this story. But they certainly ought to know it better than I. Because I was just a little girl. I don't know how young I was, but I know that my husband was old enough to be in the posse that looked for him. So he was very young. And not over 15, but I think more like 30 to 14. So it was a long time ago. And we should be able to get the dates of that from anybody who has any notes on that undertaking. Pine Creek Dairy Company.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Creamery. Creamery.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And the farmers were coming in to sell the cream. They had a big thing going. They had to get a butter maker. So they got Amal Watts, a man named Amal Watts. Very young man. As I understand, he was about 28 or not over 30. I think he was under 30, but I'm not sure. Mind you, this is all hearsay and hearsay at a later date. But of course, I was an ardent listener because I was very much interested in the story. And I was little, but I was old enough to take an entrance. So they got this butter maker, and I remember seeing him. We did not sell our cream to the creamery. The folks didn't, or the milk, they didn't sell it to them. But I saw the creamery, and I didn't get to go to town very often. And then I saw this little man, the butter maker. Very much interested in the butter maker. I read a story too about a butter maker from Denmark, which of course gave me a lot of interest in the story, in looking for the man. He was a very good looking man. And he had that fresh, clear colored complexion that comes from the Scandinavian countries. But he had black hair. As I remembered, it looked black to me at least. He had dark hair, I think dark eyes. And he made a hit with the Swedish congregation at the Lutheran church. And was taken up immediately. He sang in the choir, he was a good singer. All the young ladies were dancing up to ten and twenty. But I don't believe that he ever sailed under any false colors, as I understand it. He told us right from the start that he had a wife and two children, and he was bringing them over. And of course he could make money enough to bring them over. And I don't believe that he tried to impersonate a young man or anything like that. But I remember that they said they were different girls that really were crushing on this young man. So I don't know how long he was there, but it wasn't too long. They were working hard to get business from Coeur d 'Alene. And they were expecting to hear from Coeur d 'Alene any day. And they had an unusual large lot of cream that had come in. A big lot of butter to be made. And one morning, I believe it was early in May, that there was a skiff of snow fell early in the morning. Quite a little snow. And the ground was covered in snow in Troy. As it usually is with those little skiffs of snow, but they're usually gone by noon. But the ground that morning was covered in snow, and the farmers were getting in. Of course they began coming in at about seven o 'clock. And they expected to get them when they came back from the bank. They'd go up to the bank with their check from the week before. And then when they came back from the bank and did their other little errands in town, they came back and expected to get their cream. So they kept coming in that morning. They didn't see them, but they had known of course that this was the day following the... I told you the other time. Tell me again.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: This was the day following the arrival, right?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes, the arrival of his wife and children.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: They had just come from Sweden. And I remember that I always thought it was two little boys, but I believe I was corrected in that. That it was a girl and a boy. But at least they were two older children. And they went up on the hill to see her, because he wasn't there. But the cream room was all steamed up and going, and the butter was being made, and it was being more than made. It was just being splashed around in the big bats. And everything was in order. Everything had been done. And his cap was hanging there on the wall. Well, that wasn't anything, because he had two or three of those caps that he wore around the building for changes and so on. And the white cap was a black bill. I think that's the way I remember them. And one was hanging there on the wall. But everybody had gone from all over, not only butter people and Swedish people, his friends, the Swedes and Norwegians, in town, and there were lots of them, had gone to this big party, a pound party. And everybody had sent things. My folks didn't go, but they had sent something. As I remember it, they sent lard, a bucket of lard.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: This party was for them? For him and his wife?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: It was a good will thing, and it was done very often for people who were new in a community, or sometimes if they had a misfortune or something of that sort. Sometimes it was called by some people, I don't know if it was called by everybody, a pound party, but I remember the people out in the American Ridge had sent a pound party, and somebody took in from there, and everybody in the community contributed something. Now they didn't mean just one pound, but by the pound they measured out something in pounds. Someone sent a sack of potatoes, and a sack of this and that, and a way of vegetables, and they could bring them enough food to run them for several months.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Supply.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And they took lots of things, she just had all kinds of food there. And he left, the crowd didn't break up early, and then finally they got to thinking they might be staying too late, so they hadn't broken up very early. I don't remember how late it was when they all left, it seems to me it was around midnight. They said, well let's go up and see if he's still at home, he might have come down and got the creamery going and went back up home. They thought that was possible, went up for breakfast. So they went up to see him. She was, I think, getting up, or had gotten up. And she said no, he hadn't come back. But he had told her the night before, when they retired, he said, now don't you get up in the morning. I'll get up, I always do, I go down to the creamery and get my breakfast down there. I have a package of rolls, and I have rolls and coffee, and my coffee pot is there, and I make my coffee there every morning. It's where I always eat my breakfast, and I can eat there one more morning. It won't hurt anything at all, and you don't have to get up, and I'll come up later. So don't get up, you just sleep as late as you want to, and rest as much as you can. Don't get up until the kids wake you or something like that. So she hadn't gotten up early, and she said, she wondered at him not coming because she was thinking, but she didn't really expect him to come out. She thought he'd gotten off a busy, he'd made the remark that there'd probably be a lot to do, and so she wasn't expecting him. Well he wasn't there. S .A. Anderson, the cashier at the bank, who had a great deal to do, he was also an official of the board of the creamery, the creamery board. And I'm not sure what official capacity he occupied, but because he was cashier of the bank, he was the treasurer of a lot of societies and different things like that. And he was a very popular band in town anyway. He held positions in this and that and the other thing. All over town, he had named figures very prominently when old timers get to talking about Troy. They remember S .A. Anderson. And some of his nieces and nephews still live around Troy. But he had no children of his own.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: So he went down to the creamery and several different people who were there, young people who were in that posse at least. And then I remember people telling him, they went in to look, that he sat down in the chair occupied by Watts, the manager's chair, and put the cap on. And made a few funny remarks because he was kind of a village wag, you know, who always had a, could always ad lib and say funny things. He was a humorist of sorts, as I gather from what they tell me about him, although I can't remember him.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And he said, he made some remark that other people thought was funny afterward that he made this remark about the Amal Watts who was gone. But they had such a roaring fire build up that the farmers had wondered why he had such a terribly hot fire. And some of them had gone in and found that he wasn't anywhere around the creamery because they really got worried about the fire. And it was a terribly hot fire. They remembered all this. But they said that they did everything and when the fire died down, they had even sifted the ashes looking for him.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: They looked around and someone happened to think, well, did you see any tracks? Did you see any tracks going over the depot?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, of course, by that time, everybody and his dog had walked around the creamery looking for him in vain and looking here, there, and everywhere. And people had gone out there and they had gone over to the depot. And when they inquired over at the station, no, nobody had gone on the train. Nobody had bought a ticket and gone on the train. But they did remember that there was an early freight. And that early freight train goes up all the time up the, I think we still have it. I don't know that it goes every day now, but in the busy season when they're hauling grain and so on, we have that freight line here at Whistling, sometimes, yeah. That freight train, early in the morning, it's about 4 .30, I think it goes up somewhere around there. And they used to have this big water tank right down below the railroad station. And that always stopped and got water after it had topped up the canyon from Kendry. It always stopped every morning. So they thought about that. They might have gotten on the freight. But that was getting later in the day, 10 o 'clock and later. And all those questions had been asked and I re -asked them, but of course I wasn't hurt even.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Too small.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And they found that they telephoned, they say they telephoned up the line. And telegraphed up the line too. Of course they telegraphed the station. But even they said that they telephoned, but of course I'm not sure of that. I'm not sure of any of these things. But evidently they did telegraph up there. They telegraphed, they said, to Palouse and the freight train. I don't remember what they'd said, but anyway they got no result. And they'd examined the car for where they ride the caboose. And the people who were in the caboose hadn't seen anybody come on. And nobody got off. Among the hobos they used to always ride the caboose. They couldn't get any word there. And they had the train searched, I believe they said at Oakesdale. But they had the morning train searched at Oakesdale. And they were there thinking that perhaps he might have walked out of town to get a, to get a train, to get a, to take a train now. So he wouldn't have to go in the home, at the home train place. At the home station. But he'd been there to stock up that creamery, to get the creamery going, early in the morning. So his presence was felt there. And also he had opened a bill, or a letter from the, from the company at Coeur d 'Alene from whom they were expecting a letter, giving them the order. And that was, he had it there, that was to be given to Edsy Anderson. And it, they got the order for the letter that morning and he'd opened it, it had been opened and read. And they knew he would have rejoiced over that. Well, they said they'd stick to the ashes to make sure that there was no human bone.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: You said they sent out a posse after him too?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Afterward. After the horse was stolen.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: You know.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Old saying goes. I don't know, my husband went the next day, they went up and down the canyon, between Troy and Kendrick.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: In what hiding place?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And my husband was with him and he said that, oh there were a lot of people, I don't remember how many he said, but there were a lot of people, just a lot of people went on that, went on that searching party. And they searched up and down the canyon for him. And I heard that, of course I never met Mrs. Watson. Well I was too young to even be considered. I was just interested.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: And was I interested?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Goodness, I drank up everything I heard. But, no comment that I made was ever accepted as being anything.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: But, I remember that they said that his wife said, or his wife or widow, whatever she was, that for a while she thought, when would he come home? And wouldn't they find him and bring him home?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And now, this was several days afterward and there's still waiting for him, she said now she was hoping he wouldn't come home. Because she didn't know what condition he would be in. She was afraid that he'd, for some reason. I often wondered about that. I don't know how much that comment might have meant. You know, as a matter of fact, it might have meant a lot of things. It might have meant that she was afraid he was mentally ill.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: What kind of an idea did you have at the time? Was it a total mystery to you or did you have an idea yourself of what might happen to him?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Total mystery. It was a total mystery except that I thought he got away.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I always thought he was smart and got away.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: But what would the mystery then is, why bring his wife out?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I'd bring his wife and children.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And I've asked that and my husband and I have gone over it too.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: He was interested in that. My husband was interested in that. And we went over it. His idea there was that, but I remember that was my husband's contribution to the discussion. And not mine. That well, he might have thought this. That she'd be over here now. That if she were in Sweden, they'd find him. They're like the Canadian Mounties. They get their man. And they're so well registered over there when they leave. They know who they're looking for.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: You mean they would find him over in this country?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, my husband thought so. And he was a Swedish.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Now what was the business about the tracks?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, the tracks were all too cramped over and cramped around the building. And there had been some tracks going toward the railroad station. But the snow was melting by that time. It's a skiff of snow. Early spring snow, you know. Or late spring snow, I should say. Late spring snow is early summer practically. And by eleven o 'clock, they're probably nearly all gone. There was still snow up against the buildings. There were traces of the snow, but not too much. And there were tracks, but there were several people that went to work that direction from the other side of town. They went across there. And there was traffic both ways. In that path, it went past screaming.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Wasn't enough after the tracks to be able to tell anything.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And what happened to her as far as...

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: You said she couldn't speak English at the time?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: She didn't know much English. I guess she knew a few words well.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: S .A. Anderson. Has so much to do with everything. And it is not strange. At one time, I was looking at it from the standpoint of a little girl who didn't know everything. Didn't know about conventions and rules and laws and all that. I thought there must be a mystery connected with S .A. Anderson. But there wasn't. And when I got to thinking it over and got older and saw how it was, it was only natural she'd go to him. And he was at the bank. He had everything to say about his money. He knew about how much money he had. Oh, by the way, he didn't take a dollar of his money. He hadn't withdrawn a cent.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And he'd been banked regularly. But he'd taken money to send to her. He'd take us to come over.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: He'd saved money and taken it out for the next. But he'd been putting in cents and he hadn't taken out an extra dollar. So it was a mystery. Only if I had a count.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Did they seem to the people to be very close and happy when they got together at the pound party?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Nobody reported anything wrong. If any of those gossips had seen anything, I think they would have said it. And it would have come down through the ages. And my husband was old enough to hear those things. And his mother had been at the party.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And she thought she was a good looking woman and she'd talked to her.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: She had talked to her at the party.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, you asked me a question about the people in Cambridge.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: S .A.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Anderson took her to Spokane. And told her you could get her employment, I'm sure. And I never did find out what had happened to her for many years. I mean, I didn't find out for several years. And then S .A. Anderson's niece, Al Anderson, stayed with me one winter. Took care of my youngsters and I taught school out in the country.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Out on American Ridge, I went back to American Ridge to teach school. My husband was trying to make a transfer from Kooski where we lived for 11 years over to Murch Ridge, to his father's place, with his kennel. And took a long time, seems so. But anyway, in the meantime we had to live. And I took a school and got a little house over there. And I got to Almond's day with us and she took care of my children. And she's about the age of my children, so you see she didn't know anything about them herself, personally. But she said, well, Uncle S .A. took her school candle and looked out for her. And then she married someone from the church, she had the church up there. A countryman.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Stayed around Spokane. Stayed around Spokane, lived up there as far as I know. When I told Laura, my wife, a little bit of the story, she wondered if it wouldn't have been possible that maybe if this guy had been fallen for by some of the girls around, they really had a crush on him. Maybe one of their boys might have wanted to get even with him. And get away with it. That was the one thing she could think of.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, I don't think I ever thought of that. And I think the main reason for that was that time.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes, the theory of foul play entered into it. Because they were down there and they got a man, they got an expert, who I don't know. I never did know, Paul didn't know.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: But the coroner brought down somebody from Moscow. That sifted the ashes. They suspected foul play. And that blooming hot fire, they didn't think he would have gone off and left. The drafts opened everything to leave such a horrible fire. And they never did explain that fire. I have always wondered, I even read someplace that that's not a perfect criterion. That ashes can sometimes, that things can be consumed.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: In fact, I saw on the TV once that they said, see this, it looks solid, doesn't it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, it is solid. It's an iron bar and something else. And it didn't live in the flames, it was an iron component.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: But...

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Did people remember it for long afterwards? Did it continue to be a mystery around Troy?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh yes, for a long, long time. I married in 1918.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: 1917. I married in 1917. June of 1917.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I just don't know what year that was.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I'm sure we could find that sometime.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Well, you said that the creamery didn't last for long after that, is that right?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Oh, you probably... No, it didn't.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: It went to pieces. It fell apart. And it was just going good. Naturally, you know, a new enterprise wouldn't have been paying off well yet.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: It probably wouldn't have been so soon. A new enterprise. Well, I'll stick to that after you've...

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: ... given up for me, I guess.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: They didn't have a butter maker. And I guess that experience has kind of soured them, maybe. I don't know. I couldn't tell you about how the people felt because if I'd have been a little bigger, I'd have probably tried to get into their minds. And I could tell more about what they were thinking. But I did try to cudgel my husband's brains and then his father talked freely about it. And he said, and he was... My husband's father was a very intelligent man. And of course he liked to say no foolishness about me. I wouldn't say that it'll depend on your measure of foolishness and your definition.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Might be different.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: You know, you might not all agree. But he did talk about that. And he was mystified by it completely. He said he talked, had several conversations with anyone and he considered it very intelligent.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Would you like some water?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: No, thank you.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I do. I'm gonna get it. You know, but... No, I just do that. Crumbs in my throat.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: But...

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And they speculated and even after I was married. I always liked to bring it up. But I couldn't. I brought it up a few times. So we didn't, I don't mean, we sat around and talked about the Amalot story. But every now and then in the conversation out there it came up and different comments would be made. And Paul's father was very fertile in imagination and theories and he was down there. And he thought it was foul play at first. And he was one of the ones that insisted that the ashes be sifted. And he said, oh shock! That was one of his words. He didn't say shocks. And I remember him saying, oh shock! Of course they ought to sift the ashes. But he said they should have gotten that fire down right away as soon as they could. He said, cooled off that fire and gotten in there before it had burned, had a chance to burn out everything. And he thought maybe it had burned out. And I think that Paul's father thought that Amal Watts was burned up in that place.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: If he escaped, if he got away, it seemed like the perfect escape. Because you would think if somebody was getting out he would do something like take his money out of the bag or something. Yes.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Of course, SA had no way of knowing. He hadn't been in Troy too awful long. And he wouldn't have known how big a savings he might have had with him. He might have had savings enough to make this little getaway.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: You said he was Danish, came from Denmark.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Is that correct?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, he had been educated and you know Denmark was a famous school for dairymen and butter makers.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Used to be.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And it had a famous school for butter makers. I remember that. Of course I lived in a Scandinavian area. And I heard a lot about it. I associated it with that. I wouldn't say he was a Dane, but it did seem to me that he wasn't entirely Swedish. He might have been Swedish and Dane or Danish and Swedish or something of that sort. So he was educated in Denmark. But that he had gone to that school.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: And you said that they sent all the way to Minneapolis from Troy to get him.

speaker: Sam or Laura Schrager
words: Is that what you said too?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Yes, they got him from Minneapolis.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I should wake up late and we should get going because it's getting late.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: I suppose it is. As I said, time means nothing to me. Well, anyway. We could have supper here and pick up supper and go on with that if you wanted to.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson or Mavis Lee Utley
words: Well, I can come back and I will come back.
